meta.title: Software Engineer | Distributed Systems & SaaS | SAIFCORE
meta.description: Software engineer in Dakar — Java & cloud-native: SaaS, enterprise, distributed systems that still work once the launch party ends. Senegal & international. Sharp trade-offs, less rework.
meta.ogTitle: Software Engineer — Distributed Systems & Scalable SaaS
meta.ogDescription: From architecture to production: Java, Spring, cloud-native, SaaS & enterprise. Based in Senegal; works nationally and internationally. Focus on systems that hold up under real load and roadmap pressure.
meta.twitterDescription: Java & cloud-native · SaaS & enterprise · Dakar — systems built for week two, not just demo day.
meta.jsonLdDescription: Software engineer in Dakar, Senegal. Designs and evolves business-critical systems with Java and cloud-native architectures — SaaS, enterprise, and distributed services. Open to national engagements in Senegal and international collaboration (remote, hybrid, or on-site when appropriate).
meta.manifestName: SAIFCORE — Software Engineer | Distributed Systems & SaaS
meta.manifestDescription: Java & cloud-native systems for SaaS & enterprise — architecture through production. Senegal & international.

hero.subtitle: Built to survive launch week — and the roadmap that follows. Architecture through production: Java, cloud-native, SaaS, and platforms where downtime or bad data cost real money.
hero.locationLine: Dakar, Senegal — on-site or hybrid locally; remote or hybrid internationally. Overlap-friendly across Francophone Africa, Europe, and US time zones when it matters.
hero.nextSteps: Intro call → written scope & constraints → proposal or kickoff. No endless email tennis.
hero.jumpToContactHint: email, CV, and full booking options.

trust.heading: Trusted by teams who needed production software — not another deck

whatISolve.title: What I solve
whatISolve.subtitle: Leverage for product and engineering: ownership people can name, change that doesn’t break the world, ops you can sleep through, and dates that still mean something when traffic spikes.

items.title: Architecture & system design
items.description: Domains and contracts your team can carry — so the next feature doesn’t become a six-month refactor.

items.title: Scalable SaaS & enterprise platforms
items.description: Multi-tenant models, integrations, and how you’ll actually run it: fast now without a structure you’ll regret at Series B.

items.title: Distributed & business-critical systems
items.description: Money paths, latency budgets, audits — payments, FinTech-style flows, regulated contexts; engineered, not improvised.

items.title: Engineering excellence
items.description: Tests, CI/CD, observability, and performance baked into delivery — predictable releases instead of weekend salvages.

howIThink.title: How I think
howIThink.subtitle: From fuzzy ask to system in production — with the lightest process that still fits your risk profile.

steps.title: Frame the problem
steps.body: Who decides, what’s non-negotiable, what “good” means — before we pick a framework logo.

steps.title: Shape the architecture
steps.body: Boundaries, interfaces, and a plausible next year so your team isn’t hostage to my first diagram.

steps.title: Ship with guardrails
steps.body: Tests, CI, and alerts aligned with how you’ll really run it — not how the demo looked.

steps.title: Harden against reality
steps.body: Measure and fix where users and revenue hurt — not where the Grafana chart is prettiest.

howIThink.operatingBody: Architecture is a product: written decisions when they reduce risk, measurable outcomes, and space for your team to improve the system without firefighting.
bullets: ADRs and diagrams when they prevent expensive misunderstandings
SLOs, tracing, and alerts tied to real user journeys
Security and compliance as design inputs — not a late panic

featuredProjects.title: Featured projects
featuredProjects.subtitle: Delivery under pressure — public where possible, anonymized where confidentiality wins. Each card is a class of problem I’ve already shipped through.
featuredProjects.bridgeTitle: Seeing the same pressure on your side?
featuredProjects.bridgeSubtitle: Share goals and hard constraints — I’ll sketch scope, risks, and order of attack before anyone signs a blank check.

items.title: Ecom 360 PME
items.context: SaaS for retail and commerce — stock, sales, and daily ops in one product so SMEs can grow without bolting on five tools.
items.impact: Merchants productive in days, not months — onboarding that dodges a big-bang IT programme.

items.title: Multi-tenant B2B SaaS
items.context: Greenfield B2B SaaS — org isolation, roles, usage-based billing, and a domain model you can split as you grow.
items.impact: ~40% faster feature cadence once modular domains landed — fewer cross-team merges per release.

experience.title: Experience
experience.subtitle: Banking cores, fintech, and high-traffic products — shipping with Java/Spring, Angular, and React in regulated, production-heavy environments.
experience.valueBody: Jasper-backed reporting, mobile-money integrations, GitLab-driven delivery, and Angular/Spring at scale — hands-on production work, not architecture theatre.

items.company: BANK OF AFRICA — BOA Services International
items.bullet0: Critical-path Java/Spring banking modules for analytics and operational reporting — scalability, performance, and resilience as first-class constraints.
items.bullet1: Jasper Reports for decision-grade reporting; latency and availability work paired with business stakeholders on complex requirements.
items.bullet2: Java, Spring Boot, Jasper Reports, REST APIs, Docker, CI/CD, distributed architecture, Agile

items.bullet0: Credit and digital-transaction platform with strict security and performance targets; owned the end-to-end credit application flow.
items.bullet1: Wave & Orange Money integrations (~60% faster processing); GitLab CI/CD (~50% faster deploys); unit, integration, and regression coverage; close product partnership.
items.bullet2: Java, Spring Boot, Spring Security, PostgreSQL, GitLab CI/CD, Docker, REST APIs, Swagger, Scrum

items.bullet0: High-traffic fintech for SMEs and informal retailers; Angular UIs for stock and order management.
items.bullet1: Frontend performance (lazy loading, code splitting, caching; ~60% faster); Spring services (~30% faster API responses); UX refresh and technical docs.
items.bullet2: Angular, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, Java, Spring Boot, REST APIs, MySQL, Git, Agile

items.bullet0: Integrated hospital information system (clinic, pharmacy, lab) — Angular, Spring Boot, REST.
items.bullet1: Caching and asynchronous processing; code review and team engineering standards.
items.bullet2: Angular, Spring Boot, REST APIs, asynchronous processing, testing

architectureExpertise.title: Core expertise
architectureExpertise.subtitle: The stack and practices I reach for to get from whiteboard to on-call without drama.

about.title: About
about.p1: I connect product intent with software that can evolve: explicit trade-offs, clear boundaries, and architectures your team can still change a year after go-live.
about.p2b: architecture decisions, system design, and engineering practices
about.p2c: that keep delivery calm when complexity and traffic grow.
about.p3: Strong Java & Spring background, modern cloud-native stacks, and Angular or React when the product needs them — for SaaS, enterprise platforms, and distributed services.
about.p4: Focus: FinTech, SaaS, e-commerce, digital platforms, and large-scale enterprise systems. Based in Dakar — national (Senegal) and international collaboration: async-first, clear written specs, and live overlap when decisions need it.

insights.title: Insights & thinking
insights.subtitle: Trade-offs, failure modes, delivery — fewer pretty diagrams, more things that stay up.
insights.endCtaTitle: Facing a similar challenge in your stack?
insights.endCtaSubtitle: Use these as a lens — then we pressure-test them against your real constraints.

articlesPage.metaDescription: Practical notes on architecture, SaaS, and delivery — ideas to contrast with your own constraints.
articlesPage.title: Articles
articlesPage.subtitle: Essays, code, ADRs, and design notes — one place to steal ideas from responsibly.
articlesPage.endCtaTitle: Want to apply this to your product?
articlesPage.endCtaSubtitle: Share your stack, constraints, and timeline — I’ll say clearly if there’s a fit.

cta.title: Tell me what you’re building.
cta.subtitle: Senegal or abroad — what’s the product, what’s stuck, and what’s the timeline. You’ll get a straight answer: fit, a concrete next step, or a clear no.
cta.processStep1: You book a slot or send a brief — context, goals, and timeline.
cta.processStep2: I come back with clarifying questions and, when it fits, a lean scope or options.
cta.processStep3: We align on the next step: discovery workshop, architecture sprint, or phased delivery.
cta.responseHint: I usually acknowledge messages within two business days (often faster).
